2605.12745.title: What Do You Think I Think? Accounting for Human Beliefs Using Second-Order Theory of Mind
2605.12745.paper_summary: Discrepancies between an agent's actual knowledge and what a person thinks the agent knows can hinder interactions. If an agent could detect such discrepancies, it could provide feedback to account for them and improve current and future interactions. Using the I-POMDP as a framework for a second-order Theory of Mind (ToM-2), this work endows an agent with the ability to model the evolution of a person's erroneous beliefs about an agent and the cognitive biases and heuristics (CBH) from which they arise. In doing so, the agent can detect when CBH might be at play during an interaction and adaptively generate feedback that accounts for them. An in-person user study shows how a ToM-2 learner can account for the effects of a teacher's CBH to significantly improve the informativeness of teacher actions, and subjective results suggest people find the ToM-2 learner's feedback more useful.
2605.12745.paper_summary_zh: 代理人實際知識與個體認為代理人所知之間的差異可能會妨礙互動。<lb/>如果代理人能夠檢測到這些差異，它可以提供反饋來考慮這些差異，並改善當前和未來的互動。<lb/>本研究使用I-POMDP作為第二階段心智理論（ToM-2）的框架，使代理人具備建模個體對代理人錯誤信念演變的能力，以及產生這些信念的認知偏見和啟發式（CBH）。<lb/>這樣一來，代理人可以在互動過程中檢測到CBH可能發揮作用的時機，並自適應地生成考慮到這些因素的反饋。<lb/>一項面對面的用戶研究顯示，ToM-2學習者如何考慮教師CBH的影響，顯著提高教師行為的資訊性，並且主觀結果表明，人們認為ToM-2學習者的反饋更有用。
2605.12745.author: Patrick Callaghan et.al.
2605.12745.authors: Patrick Callaghan, Reid Simmons, Henny Admoni
